note_data.title: Further organic chemistry
keyPoints: **Benzene** has a delocalised π electron system with all C-C bonds equal in length and shows resistance to addition reactions
**Phenol** is weakly acidic due to delocalisation of the lone pair on oxygen into the benzene ring, forming a phenoxide ion
**Primary amines** can be formed by reduction of nitriles using LiAlH4 or by nucleophilic substitution of haloalkanes with ammonia
**Amides** are formed from acyl chlorides and amines, and can be hydrolysed under acidic or basic conditions to form carboxylic acids
**Amino acids** are amphoteric compounds that can exist as zwitterions at their isoelectric point
**Proteins** have primary structure (amino acid sequence), secondary structure (α-helix or β-pleated sheet) and tertiary structure (3D folding)
**Polyesters** are formed by condensation polymerisation between dicarboxylic acids and diols with elimination of water
**Polyamides** are formed by condensation polymerisation between dicarboxylic acids and diamines or by ring-opening of lactams

definitions.definition: The spreading of electron density over more than two atoms in a molecule or ion
An ion that has both a positive and negative charge on different atoms in the same molecule
A reaction where monomers join together with the elimination of small molecules such as water
The pH at which an amino acid exists predominantly as a zwitterion with no net charge
An amide link formed between the amino group of one amino acid and the carboxyl group of another

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
For benzene, always show delocalised structure with circle. For amino acids, show zwitterion form at isoelectric point
Use evidence given to work out structure. Show your working clearly

modelAnswer.question: Explain why phenol is more acidic than ethanol. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Phenol can lose H⁺ from the -OH group to form a phenoxide ion. The negative charge on the oxygen atom is delocalised into the benzene ring. This delocalisation stabilises the phenoxide ion, making it easier for phenol to lose H⁺ and act as an acid. In ethanol, there is no delocalisation so the ethoxide ion is less stable.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Phenol loses H⁺ to form phenoxide ion
Mark 2: Negative charge is delocalised into benzene ring
Mark 3: Delocalisation stabilises the ion / makes phenol more acidic

commonMistakes: Never say benzene has 'alternating single and double bonds' — always refer to delocalised π electrons
Students often forget that amino acids exist as zwitterions in neutral conditions, not as molecules with -NH2 and -COOH
Don't confuse addition polymerisation with condensation polymerisation — polyesters and polyamides eliminate small molecules
Never say phenol is acidic because of the benzene ring — it's because the phenoxide ion is stabilised by delocalisation
Students often draw peptide bonds incorrectly — remember it's specifically the amide linkage -CO-NH-
examinerTips: Benzene reactions appear almost every year in Paper 4 — practice drawing mechanisms
Always show the zwitterion structure when asked about amino acids at physiological pH
For polymerisation questions, clearly show the repeating unit in brackets with continuation bonds
Protein structure questions often ask for specific examples — learn α-helix and β-pleated sheet
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Benzene delocalised structure showing circle inside hexagon, zwitterion structure of amino acids (H3N⁺-CHR-COO⁻), and peptide bond formation between amino acids with water elimination
